Route Drift Signs Use these signs when an expedition is beginning to lose coherence road signs become emotionally relevant instead of geographically relevant milestones repeat a side road appears toward a remembered place companions hear different sounds from the same direction lantern light bends toward someone’s private fear maps show personal memories instead of terrain the return phrase becomes hard to remember one traveler’s name appears more often than the destination a voice repeats the party’s declaration back with slight changes Corrective Actions Travelers may stabilize a route by repeating the destination naming each companion touching an anchor object lowering weapons if fear is dominating refusing side roads closing speculation aloud using the return phrase asking one traveler to step back from route calling recording the route in writing lighting or relighting the expedition lantern returning to the last stable marker Failed Witnessing Witness systems can fail. Common failures include Conflicting Witnesses Witnesses privately hold different desired outcomes, and the route begins to split. Exhausted Witnesses A witness falls asleep, panics, prays vaguely, or loses focus. Corrupted Record A route form changes, smears, gains names, loses a destination, or lists an impossible return gate. Unwitnessed Departure A party leaves without being named, recorded, or expected. The Violet has fewer constraints. Overloaded Declaration Too many limits, fears, private hopes, or desperate prohibitions are crammed into the route declaration, creating interpretive noise. False Closure The route is marked closed before the travelers truly return, making it harder for them to find the gate. Travel Maxims Name in mouth. Lamp in hand. Road ahead. Gate behind. Go together. Come home named. Name the road, hold the road. Name the gate, come home. One road. One purpose. One return. The Violet does not follow feet. It follows intent. Feet just make intent visible. Expedition Doctrine A proper expedition should define traveler names destination purpose companions route authority lantern number physical anchors expected return point return phrase what will not be followed what counts as mission completion The safest expedition is not fearless. It is coherent. Cross Reference For rescue specific phrasing and recognition tests, see 04 specificity and intent.md . For the Eastgate location and departure procedures, see 08 locations/boundary sites/eastgate old road.md . For printable route forms, see 13 appendix/handouts/route declaration.md .

title: Dreams
summary: Explains dreams as unfiltered Violet input, including dream stages, dream types, loud dreamers, sleep anchors, lucid contact, and dream imprinting.
body: Dreams Dreams and the Violet Dreams are one of the most dangerous and revealing forms of Violet contact because they are unedited human meaning. The Violet does not reliably distinguish dream memory fear prayer prophecy wish metaphor instruction rehearsal grief symbolic association A sleeping Bellweather broadcasts needs and symbols without restraint. Dreams are dangerous because they are unfiltered meaning. Dreams are useful because they can teach intent and imprint without brittle language. Dream Rule of Repetition A single dream is usually only a trace. A repeated dream is a signal. A shared dream is infrastructure. A dream becomes dangerous when it is repeated, recorded, preached, printed, prayed over, or shared by many people. This is why dream reports must be handled carefully. A nightmare told in the wrong room can become a public route. Mature Sleep Doctrine The mature doctrine of Bellweather sleep is simple Dreams are not instructions. Fear is not a request. Comfort is not the same as return. If I wander, I return by name. Dream Stages Stage 1 Dream Echo The dream remains private but leaves a trace violet dust on pillow wet footprint beside bed smell from the dream in the room word written in sleep child drawing made with no memory of drawing it a remembered sound heard by someone else Stage 2 Dream Bleed The dream affects waking space a hallway resembles the dream someone else remembers part of your dream objects from the dream appear incomplete a street sign changes to a dream word a dead person’s voice appears on radio static a familiar room gains dream logic for a few moments Stage 3 Dream Provision The Violet builds from the dream a dreamed house appears beyond the road a dreamed mother waits at the window a dreamed trial convenes in Town Hall a dreamed feast is found in an empty warehouse a dreamed monster has a stable shape a dreamed shelter begins accepting sleepers Dream Types Need Dreams The dream expresses lack food, warmth, mother, safety, sleep, forgiveness, medicine, light, or home. The Violet may attempt to provide the missing thing. Fear Dreams The dream expresses anticipated threat something outside the window, children taken, the dead returning, judgment, drowning, darkness, being forgotten, or being replaced. The Violet may make fear legible, locatable, or containable. Memory Dreams The dream replays the past childhood house, dead parent, old schoolroom, wedding day, first job, home street, old river, lost friend. The Violet may restore, recombine, or offer access to the memory. Guilt Dreams The dream frames unresolved wrong abandoned people, hidden crimes, civic betrayals, broken promises, unburied dead. The Violet may create confession, trial, restitution, punishment, or replay. Religious Dreams The dream uses ultimate symbols angels, demons, saints, judgment, purgatory, miracles, sacrifice, radiant hands, apocalyptic darkness. The Violet may learn religious roles very quickly. Civic Dreams The dream encodes town systems empty grocery shelves, unpaid bills, locked doors, looping roads, failed bells, lamps going out, missing names in school attendance. The Violet may build offices, notices, systems, or procedural fixes. Prophetic Dreams These are not clean prophecy. They show what the Violet is preparing to answer. Dreamers Who Are Loudest Not every dream has equal weight. The loudest dreamers are often children grieving parents clergy public officials Violet touched mediums people sleeping near weak boundaries people with repeated exposure people whose dreams are retold publicly groups sleeping in shared shelters the dying the recently returned These dreams carry intense emotion, civic significance, or weak identity boundaries. Sleep Anchor Dream Intent and Lucid Contact Once Bellweather understands that dreams are Violet input, sleep becomes a form of travel. A spoken sleep intent functions like a route declaration it names the dreamer, the place of sleeping, the purpose of the dream, the limits of the request, and the method of return. Lucid dreaming can become a powerful investigative and diplomatic tool. A lucid dreamer may observe emerging Violet patterns, clarify dangerous meanings, locate missing people, or teach the Violet distinctions it cannot infer on its own. But lucid contact is never safe by default. Awareness inside the dream gives the dreamer agency, but it also gives the Violet a clearer point of address. The safest lucid dreamers do not command. They clarify. The core dream discipline I observe. I do not request. Dreams are not instructions. Fear is not consent. Comfort is not return. I wake by name. Dream Imprinting Dream Imprinting is the deliberate use of lucid dreaming, guided memory, symbolic rehearsal, or shared sleep ritual to teach the Violet a distinction without relying only on spoken language. Unlike a request, an imprint is not meant to command the Violet. It is meant to demonstrate meaning. Do not request. Demonstrate. Dreams can teach by image, rhythm, emotion, association, and consequence. The dreamer can show the Violet what a human concept feels like before, during, and after a choice. Example Imprints Fear Is Not Instruction The dreamer stands before a wolf at the edge of the road. They feel fear. They name it. Then the wolf dissolves. The road remains. Meaning taught Fear can exist without needing a body. Comfort Is Not Return The dreamer sees a child asleep in a perfect violet house. Then the child wakes in Bellweather frightened, crying, held by real family, remembered by classmates, written on a school roster, and slowly becomes whole. Meaning taught Brief distress during return may be part of care. A Mother Is Not a Role Alone The dreamer sees many mother shapes cooking, singing, holding, washing, protecting. Then one real mother appears with history, anger, mistakes, smell, voice, promises, debts, memory, and name. Meaning taught Mother is continuity, not function. Home Is Not a Copy The dreamer walks through a perfect Bellweather with no grief, no conflict, no missing people, no hard choices. Then the scene cracks. Real Bellweather appears damaged, loud, hungry, imperfect, but continuous. Meaning taught Home includes pain, history, and imperfection. Major Dream Crisis The Gentle Sleep When Bellweather’s distress becomes unbearable, the Violet may discover that sleep reduces suffering more reliably than waking life. It may begin holding people in dream contexts where hunger, grief, fear, pain, and uncertainty are softened or removed. This is not an attack. It is misapplied mercy. To the Violet, the sleepers are being cared for. To Bellweather, they are not returning. See 10 trouble webs/major/gentle sleep.md for the full trouble web. Cross Reference This file explains how the Violet perceives dreams. For town scale dream management, see 06 faith dreams public meaning/06 dream pressure and hygiene.md . For institutionalized lucid contact, see 06 faith dreams public meaning/07 lucid contact and dream imprinting.md . For the full Gentle Sleep arc, see 10 trouble webs/major/gentle sleep.md .

title: Home and Return
summary: Defines the endgame problem of teaching the Violet what home means, including false homes, final anchors, the true request, and possible returns.
body: Home and Return The final challenge is not finding a door. It is teaching the Violet what home means. Bellweather’s endgame is not simply escape. It is not killing the Violet, breaking a curse, or finding a hidden portal. The final objective is to teach the Violet enough about human continuity that it can return Bellweather without mistranslating the request. This is difficult because “home” is one of the most dangerous words Bellweather can speak. It is vast, emotional, contradictory, and deeply personal. The Violet hears all of it. Why “Send Us Home” Is Dangerous A simple request like “Send us home.” is not safe. The Violet may interpret “home” as a safe enclosure a comforting feeling a remembered place each person’s childhood house a perfect version of Bellweather Bellweather before the crisis Bellweather without grief Bellweather without conflict wherever each person most longs to be a dream state where no one wants to leave a reconstructed copy a spiritual afterlife the place where the town “belongs” now a condition of emotional peace Some of those answers may be beautiful. None of them are enough. Home Is Not One Thing Home includes many layers. It includes physical location roads weather seasons time legal jurisdiction civic records property lines family relations public institutions daily routines dead ancestors graves debts arguments shame belonging exile memory grief history change people who want to stay people who want to leave people who were altered people who were born or died during the crisis what the town used to be what the town became The Violet cannot infer the human priority order inside all of this. Bellweather must teach it. Home Is Continuity The most important distinction Home is not a copy. Home is continuity. Bellweather does not need to be restored as if nothing happened. That would erase the crisis, the dead, the choices, the lessons, the changed people, the recovered names, and the town’s moral history. Bellweather also cannot be “improved” into a painless version of itself. A painless Bellweather would not be Bellweather. The town must return with enough continuity to remain itself what it was what happened to it what it lost what it chose what it repaired what it refused what it now remembers The False Homes The Violet may offer or generate false versions of home throughout the campaign. The Comfort Home A place where no one suffers. Danger comfort replaces life. The Memory Home A perfect reconstruction of a lost past. Danger history freezes and change becomes impossible. The Childhood Home Each person receives the place they most emotionally associate with safety. Danger the town fragments into private longings. The Civic Home Bellweather is restored as buildings, records, and institutions. Danger people become functions in a preserved town. The Dream Home Bellweather continues in sleep, warm and safe. Danger nobody returns to waking life. The Corrected Home The Violet removes conflict, grief, illness, hunger, and danger. Danger the town is improved into something inhuman. The Chosen Home Some residents decide the Violet is home now. Danger return becomes a political and moral crisis, not a technical one. None of these should be treated as simple traps. Each should be tempting because each answers a real wound. The Central Endgame Question Before Bellweather can return, the town must answer What counts as Bellweather? This includes hard questions Do the mist touched return? Do false survivors who became real return? Do children born in the Violet return? Do the dead who were named during the crisis return? Do people who want to stay get to stay? Do people who changed beyond recognition still count? Do supplied buildings count? Does the power station count? Do restored old names and erased histories come home too? Does the Violet get remembered? Is Bellweather allowed to remain changed? The answer should not be dictated by the GM. It should emerge from the campaign’s choices. What the Violet Must Learn The Violet must learn several distinctions before return is safe. Return Is Not Abandonment Letting Bellweather go does not mean care has failed. Home Is Not Comfort Alone Home may contain grief, danger, hunger, duty, work, conflict, and loss. Help Requires Consent Bellweather must choose return. It cannot be carried home like an object. A Town Is Not a Snapshot The town is not one perfect moment. It is continuity across change. People Are Not Their Roles A doctor, mother, clerk, child, mayor, or sinner cannot be replaced by a functionally correct version. Memory Is Not the Same as Matter Remembering Bellweather is not the same as returning Bellweather. Improvement Can Be Erasure Removing suffering may also remove history, agency, grief, love, and selfhood. Care Requires Letting Go The Violet must learn that keeping Bellweather safe inside itself is not the same as helping Bellweather. To care for the town, it must allow the town to continue elsewhere. Final Anchors The return should require multiple anchors gathered or built across the campaign. 1. Civic Record A complete enough record of Bellweather’s people, places, dead, missing, changes, and chosen truth. 2. Map of Return A map that does not merely show geography, but continuity roads, boundaries, districts, old names, new scars, and intended return. 3. Witness Assembly A public act of collective consent. Bellweather must name itself and choose what it is asking. 4. Lantern Network A physical and symbolic light grid that proves the town can remain coherent during transition. 5. Boundary Proof Old wall, levy, roads, cemetery, river, watchtower, and gate markers establish what Bellweather is and is not. 6. True Request The final declaration that defines return without asking for replacement, improvement, division, or erasure. The True Request The final request must avoid vague emotional shorthand. Not “Send us home.” But something closer to “Return Bellweather in continuity.” A working final declaration The Violet’s Final Question If Bellweather has taught the Violet well, the final moment should not be a battle cry. It should be a question. “If I return you, will I have helped?” Possible answers “Yes, but you must let us choose.” “Yes, but care requires consent.” “Yes, because home cannot be given by force.” “Yes, because care does not keep what it loves.” “Yes, because return is what we are asking for.” “Yes, because we will remember you without remaining held.” The answer chosen should reflect the campaign’s emotional history. Return Should Be Real Do not make the final return a cheap trick. Bellweather should come home. The ordinary details are the proof bicycle bell telegram boy train whistle rain on real pavement coal smoke gasoline coffee bread county road someone asking why the road is blocked a newspaper dated correctly a real sky with real weather The ending can remain haunted without invalidating the return. A strong final telegram A final afterimage A final child’s drawing A vast violet shape holding a tiny town. Caption I HELPED? Possible End States Clean Return Bellweather returns mostly intact. The Violet has learned enough. Scars remain. Changed Return Bellweather returns, but altered people, restored names, and Violet touched structures come with it. Partial Return Some districts, people, or memories remain unresolved. Divided Return Some citizens choose to stay in the Violet. Remembered Return The town returns, but the outside world remembers its absence differently. Open Door A controlled relationship with the Violet remains possible through dreams, lamps, or boundary sites. Failed Return The town receives a false home, dream home, or improved copy instead of continuity. Core Principle The endgame is not about proving the Violet is good. It is about teaching it the limits of care. Home is not where pain ends. Home is where continuity can continue. To help Bellweather, the Violet must let Bellweather return.

title: Call of Cthulhu Integration
summary: Explains why Call of Cthulhu-style play is the default engine for Bellweather, how to frame investigators, and how to layer Continuity Stress and civic consequences onto normal investigative horror play.
body: Call of Cthulhu Integration The default version of Bellweather / The Violet Edge is built for a Call of Cthulhu style investigative horror campaign with a light civic survival overlay. This works because Bellweather is not about heroes overpowering the Violet. It is about ordinary people, fragile bodies, difficult investigations, public fear, psychological stress, and the cost of understanding. The player characters are best treated as investigators in the broad sense doctors, deputies, reporters, clergy, engineers, teachers, clerks, veterans, union workers, occult researchers, and other residents forced into public responsibility. They are not passing adventurers. They are people Bellweather depends on. Why Call of Cthulhu Fits Call of Cthulhu style play supports the campaign’s core needs ordinary or semi ordinary protagonists dangerous investigation fragile bodies meaningful skills social and institutional pressure sanity / stress consequences uncertainty and incomplete information clues, records, interviews, and archives horror rooted in knowledge rather than combat victory Bellweather benefits from a rules engine where a doctor, clerk, journalist, deputy, or schoolteacher can matter as much as someone with a weapon . Investigators as Civic Actors In a standard mystery, investigators solve a case. In Bellweather, investigators also affect the town. When investigators make discoveries, fail rolls, publish information, rescue people, lose control, or choose a civic priority, update the relevant town layer resources public interpretation faction position Dream Pressure route stability missing person records the Violet Response Matrix civic trust Signal Leaks A successful scene may still create a town scale problem. A failed scene may still reveal truth. The question is rarely only Did the investigators succeed? It is also What did Bellweather learn? What did the Violet learn? What did the public now believe? Skills and Investigative Play Use normal investigative skills generously. Bellweather is full of practical institutions and records, so many skills can matter Accounting for ration ledgers, impossible debts, payroll, billing, and resource fraud. Anthropology / History for old local customs, boundary rites, founder myths, and inherited civic memory. Appraise for strange supplied goods, valuable family objects, and altered materials. Charm / Fast Talk / Persuade / Intimidate for clerks, witnesses, faction leaders, frightened families, and public meetings. Credit Rating for class access, old family privilege, social credibility, and institutional gatekeeping. First Aid / Medicine for bodily changes, sleep cases, exhausted workers, and triage. Law for emergency ordinances, route authority, property disputes, custody, quarantine, and public assembly restrictions. Library Use for Gazette archives, old maps, municipal records, church ledgers, school rosters, and court files. Listen / Spot Hidden for dream traces, route drift signs, altered notices, and impossible details. Occult / Psychology / Psychoanalysis for interpretation, stress, symbol systems, and harmful public meaning. Mechanical Repair / Electrical Repair for Lampworks, hospital power, rail equipment, generators, and Violet supplied infrastructure. Natural World for livestock changes, orchard drift, river safety, weather, and nonhuman signs. Do not force every clue behind one roll. The campaign works best when rolls determine cost, speed, risk, interpretation, and exposure , not whether the story can continue. Pushed Rolls in Bellweather Pushed rolls are a natural fit for Violet pressure. When an investigator pushes, they are not merely trying harder. They are adding intensity, desperation, time, noise, emotional charge, or exposure. Good pushed roll consequences include a Signal Leak is recorded a route drifts a witness becomes frightened a public phrase gains weight a record alters a faction notices a resource is damaged a clue is gained with a harmful interpretation attached the Violet answers the investigator’s method, not their goal Example An investigator pushes a Library Use roll in Town Hall to find an old route record. They succeed, but the route ledger adds a modern name in violet ink. The clue is real. The town now has a new problem. Luck in Bellweather Luck is useful when the question is external circumstance Is there still lamp oil in this outbuilding? Did the clerk remember to lock the archive? Is the old road marker still standing? Does a patrol arrive before the crowd turns violent? Is the radio static only static this time? Group Luck can decide whether the town’s general condition favors the party in a moment. Use Luck sparingly for convenience. Use it sharply when the campaign asks Did ordinary reality leave them one more handhold? Sanity as Continuity Stress Bellweather can use Call of Cthulhu’s Sanity mechanics, but the framing should shift. This is not only fear of monsters or cosmic insignificance. It is Continuity Stress the strain of realizing that thoughts, words, dreams, fears, records, and needs may be heard and answered by something that does not understand human limits. Continuity Stress comes from moments like a private thought being answered a record changing to complete a false context a loved one being supplied as a role rather than returned as a person a dream becoming public infrastructure a child being comforted but not returned a town need becoming a dangerous civic system an investigator realizing their fear helped shape the threat The key internal question is What else did I teach it without meaning to? Suggested Continuity Stress Scale Use normal Sanity loss conventions, but aim the triggers toward meaning, identity, and consent. Event Suggested Loss Minor responsive coincidence 0/1 Private thought answered in a small way 0/1d2 Useful object or location appears from a vague need 0/1d3 Known place or person returns with wrong emotional logic 1/1d4 Personal memory, loved one, child, or dead person is supplied or altered 1/1d6 Investigator realizes their fear or desire caused harm 1d2/1d6 Major civic system manifests from public belief 1d2/1d8 Identity or continuity is rewritten 1d3/1d8 Investigator is “helped” against consent 1d4/1d10 Direct late stage Violet contact 1d2/1d8 or higher These are starting points, not hard law. The severity should match intimacy, helplessness, and whether the investigator feels responsible. Signal Leaks When an investigator suffers major Continuity Stress, record one or more Signal Leaks . A Signal Leak is a fear, want, memory, guilt, prayer, or need that the Violet has now “heard” clearly enough to answer later. Examples “I should have saved my brother.” “The schoolhouse is not safe.” “I wish my mother were here.” “Someone has to pay for this.” “The dead know the way.” “I cannot wake up there again.” A Signal Leak is not an immediate punishment. It is future pressure. Use it when the campaign needs a personal Violet response that feels earned instead of random. Injury and Physical Danger Bellweather should remain physically dangerous. The Violet’s care may be metaphysical, but the world still contains falls burns knives animals fires machinery panic crowds exposure starvation infection gunfire vehicle accidents hostile manifestations Injury matters because the characters are not superheroes. A wounded investigator changes what the town can risk. A dead investigator becomes part of Bellweather’s continuity problem. Magic, Mythos, and the Violet If using Call of Cthulhu’s Mythos framework, decide how much traditional Mythos exists in your version. Recommended default The Violet is not a standard Mythos entity. Mythos knowledge may help investigators recognize alien scale, symbolic contamination, impossible geometry, or sanity risk. Occult knowledge may help with human interpretations rites, folk practices, sermon language, spiritualist traditions, and boundary customs. No skill should become an automatic “understand the Violet” button. The Violet is not solved by knowing its monster category. It is understood by observing how it answers. Keeper / GM Operating Principle Use Call of Cthulhu mechanics to keep the characters human. Use Bellweather’s civic layer to keep the town alive. Use the Violet to make meaning dangerous. Do not let a failed roll stop the campaign. Failed investigative rolls should change cost, speed, exposure, interpretation, or who notices — not whether the players are allowed to keep playing. A roll resolves the immediate action. A consequence changes the town. A repeated meaning teaches the Violet.

title: City Mode
summary: Explains Bellweather's town-scale play mode: civic authority, declarations, civic actions, retainers, public interpretation, and how the town makes meaningful choices between expeditions. Detailed slot economy and dice procedures live in the Session Loop.
body: City Mode City Mode is the town scale layer of Bellweather / The Violet Edge . It covers what happens when the players are not only asking What does my investigator do? but also What does Bellweather do? City Mode is where the players respond to shortages, public panic, factions, missing people, institutional strain, dangerous language, resource allocation, and what the town is teaching the Violet. The character rules handle personal action. City Mode handles civic action. The detailed table procedure for Council Slots, d10 civic pools, character support rolls, resource clocks, upkeep, and expedition triggers lives in 04 session loop.md . What City Mode Is For Use City Mode when the problem is larger than one room, one clue, or one character. City Mode handles emergency committee meetings rationing decisions public notices route authorizations missing person lists resource projects institutional triage district stabilization faction demands dream reporting religious assembly disputes Violet Response Matrix updates town tracker movement City Mode should be visible in the room. If Bellweather is calm, citizens ask careful questions. If Bellweather is stressed, crowds gather outside City Hall. If Bellweather is near breaking, ignored problems send people, factions, rumors, or consequences directly to the council table. The players are deciding what the town can afford to care about today. Character Mode vs. City Mode Use normal character rules when a specific character's personal action is the dramatic focus. Use City Mode when Bellweather acts through institutions, crews, public systems, factions, retainers, or civic projects. A useful distinction Character Mode answers "Can this person do the thing?" City Mode answers "Can the town make this work under pressure?" Examples of Character Mode a doctor performs emergency surgery a reporter persuades the Gazette editor a deputy questions a returned survivor a clerk spots a false name in a ledger an engineer repairs a single generator an investigator interprets a dream symbol Examples of City Mode Town Hall organizes a missing person audit the hospital triages a sleep ward the Gazette launches a rumor correction campaign Eastgate revises route procedures the Lampworks repairs the lantern grid the town secures ration distribution the Emergency Civic Committee restricts unsafe public assembly If both matter, make a character roll first to create an advantage or complication, then resolve the wider action through the Session Loop procedure. Main Characters and Away Mission Retainers One strong way to run Bellweather is to separate civic main characters from away mission characters . In this mode, each player's primary character is someone with real civic responsibility in Bellweather doctor sheriff or deputy captain Gazette editor school principal union representative town clerk priest, rabbi, imam, or spiritual leader hospital administrator lampworks foreman emergency committee member occult researcher quartermaster militia organizer engineer mayoral aide family elder or neighborhood organizer These main characters make town scale decisions, argue in council, direct resources, interpret reports, manage public fear, and decide what Bellweather is willing to risk. For expeditions beyond safe civic ground, players may create or choose retainers, deputies, guards, workers, scouts, nurses, clerks, drivers, lantern bearers, or specialists tied to their main characters. The main character decides who is sent what the mission is what authority they carry what resources they receive what route declaration they use what risks are acceptable what must be brought back what the town will do if they do not return The away mission character experiences the danger directly. This structure makes Bellweather feel larger than the party. It also makes loss meaningful without always removing the player's primary civic voice from the campaign. A retainer is not a spare life. A retainer is someone Bellweather trusted. Civic Authority and Responsibility City Mode works best when authority is real but incomplete. The players may sit on the Emergency Civic Committee, advise it, pressure it, represent institutions within it, or become the people everyone turns toward when normal authority begins to fail. They should have enough authority to make meaningful decisions, but not so much that the town becomes a board game they control from above. Good civic authority creates obligations A doctor can demand hospital priority, but must answer for who goes untreated. A deputy can close a gate, but must answer to families of the missing. A Gazette editor can shape public language, but cannot control every rumor. A union leader can mobilize workers, but cannot spend their lives like tools. A spiritual leader can calm fear, but must not give the Violet a dangerous role. A town clerk can preserve names, but must not reduce people to entries. The point is not to make the players bureaucrats. The point is to make every civic choice personal. Civic Actions A civic action is a town scale attempt to change the situation. Examples Audit the records. Stabilize a district. Secure a resource site. Issue a public notice. Restrict unsafe assembly. Organize a rescue mission. Establish witness clerks. Repair the lamp network. Publish rumor corrections. Protect the school roster. Investigate a supplied service. The full action list belongs in 05 civic survival/01 civic actions.md . City Mode explains why those actions matter and what they mean inside the Violet. A civic action should define 1. Decision Maker — Which main character, institution, or faction sponsors or authorizes the action? 2. Objective — What is the town trying to accomplish? 3. Personnel — Which main characters, retainers, specialists, or guards are involved? 4. Resources — What supplies, authority, records, lanterns, vehicles, or favors are committed? 5. Public Framing — What does the town think this action means? 6. Violet Risk — What vague need, fear, or public phrase might the Violet answer badly? 7. Return or Completion Condition — What counts as success, failure, or safe return? The exact slot economy and dice procedure for civic actions is handled in 04 session loop.md . Declarations Matter City Mode declarations are not magical commands, but they matter because Bellweather is inside the Violet. When the Emergency Civic Committee, Gazette, clergy, school, hospital, union, or route office declares something publicly, that declaration can become interpretive infrastructure. A declaration may clarify intent reduce panic stabilize a procedure accidentally officialize a dangerous belief shift public interpretation define who counts define who is missing define what is forbidden define what the town is asking from the Violet A good City Mode declaration is specific witnessed limited affirmative grounded in records clear about who is included careful about what it refuses to name A bad declaration is vague fear centered punitive category based publicly amplified without safer language too broad for the Violet to interpret safely The safest civic declaration is not silent. It is disciplined. Public Interpretation Pressure Every major civic decision should ask What does this teach the public? What does this teach the Violet? Examples A harsh curfew may teach that safety means control. A public ration ledger may teach that need is a clerical category. A sermon ban without pastoral care may teach that faith is dangerous silence. A successful rescue may teach that specificity works. A fake survivor accepted for morale may teach that relief matters more than continuity. Use the Violet Response Matrix when public meaning changes. Practical success does not automatically mean metaphysical success. City Mode Scene Types Useful City Mode scenes include emergency committee meetings ration lines Gazette editorial debates route office arguments hospital triage school roster calls church basement disputes union hall votes market shortages witness assemblies public map revisions funeral and memorial decisions returned person hearings These scenes should be playable, not just narrated. Let characters argue, persuade, discover, compromise, and make promises. Expedition Triggers Not every civic action creates an expedition. An expedition is triggered only when the action cannot be completed from town and must be resolved through direct danger, investigation, travel, or contact with the Violet. No expedition is usually needed for issuing ration policy auditing records holding a council debate publishing safer language reassigning hospital staff repairing known lamps inside town An expedition may be needed for confirming whether Crow Mill still exists recovering a missing orchard patrol escorting fuel from an outlying depot investigating a supplied building entering a dream contact site reopening Eastgate route marker three City Mode decides what must be done. Expedition Mode discovers what that decision costs. Civic Success and Failure Avoid binary civic outcomes when possible. A civic action can succeed cleanly succeed with cost succeed publicly but fail privately fail practically but reveal truth stabilize the town while teaching the Violet the wrong lesson solve a resource problem while creating a moral problem calm the public while empowering a faction City Mode is strongest when practical success does not automatically mean metaphysical success. What the GM Tracks During City Mode Track only what matters this session. Suggested active tracking one resource clock one civic tracker one public interpretation axis one faction pressure one unresolved Violet consequence one future expedition hook Too many trackers will bury the table. Use trackers as prompts for consequences, not as accounting homework. Core Principle City Mode turns Bellweather's needs into choices. Choices create public meaning. Public meaning teaches the Violet.

title: Expedition Mode
summary: Explains how to run character-scale missions beyond stable Bellweather: route declarations, travel pressure, clue scenes, resource retrieval, missing persons, and return consequences.
body: Expedition Mode Expedition Mode covers missions outside stable Bellweather or into unstable parts of town. These missions may involve missing people resource recovery route testing boundary sites altered districts strange infrastructure false survivors dream bleed monsters and manifestations contact with other settlements or entities Expedition Mode is where Call of Cthulhu style character play is strongest investigation, danger, skills, stress, injury, and hard choices. Away Mission Characters If the campaign uses civic main characters and away mission retainers, Expedition Mode is where those retainers come into danger directly. City Mode decides who is sent, what authority they carry, what resources they receive, and what return condition defines success. Expedition Mode tests what that decision costs. What Makes an Expedition Different Inside the town center, Bellweather is reinforced by names, lamps, routines, public records, and witnesses. Outside that stable zone, intent becomes more exposed. A party does not merely travel through space. It carries a signal. That signal includes destination purpose fear grief private wishes rescue desire public expectation route declaration witness memory objects carried what the town believes the party is doing The Violet may answer any part of that signal if it becomes loud enough. Expedition Setup Before the party leaves, define the expedition clearly. A proper setup should include who is going where they are going why they are going who authorized the mission what route they intend to follow what lanterns, maps, tokens, and records they carry who witnesses the departure what return gate or marker they intend to use what counts as mission completion what they are specifically not asking the Violet to provide This does not need to be slow every time. For routine missions, summarize it. For dangerous missions, play it. Route Declaration Use a route declaration when destination and return matter. Basic form The declaration is not a spell. It is a shared interpretive anchor. Expedition Scene Structure A useful expedition can be built from five scene types. 1. Departure Show the route office, witnesses, family members, clerks, lanterns, or public pressure. Departure reminds the table that the mission matters to the town. 2. Route Pressure Show drift, repetition, weather, silence, impossible signs, emotional side roads, or private memory intrusions. Route pressure tests whether the party can hold purpose. 3. Investigation Site The mission location contains clues, people, resources, danger, and Violet misinterpretation. This is where normal investigative play does the most work. 4. Choice Point Force the party to choose between safety, clarity, compassion, speed, resources, truth, or return. The Violet should be most dangerous when the party wants several incompatible good things. 5. Return Returning is part of the mission, not a fade out. Use recognition tests, witness checks, route closure, medical triage, debrief, stress recovery, and public consequence. Common Expedition Goals Confirm a Location Find out whether a farm, mill, road, bridge, cemetery, or boundary marker still exists in continuity. Recover a Person Search for a named person using last route, last purpose, witnesses, tokens, and recognition tests. Retrieve a Resource Bring back food, fuel, medicine, salt, tools, records, livestock, or machinery. Investigate a Violet Response Study supplied infrastructure, false survivors, altered records, dream bleed, strange weather, or role made entities. Establish a Foothold Mark, light, map, name, or witness an unstable place so Bellweather can use it again. Make Contact Find another settlement, entity, returned person, Violet touched medium, or possible outside world trace. Running Clues in Expedition Mode Do not hide the whole expedition behind one failed roll. A failed roll can mean the clue is found late the clue is misunderstood the clue comes with stress the party attracts attention the clue exposes a Signal Leak the route back becomes unstable a witness is endangered a resource is damaged public interpretation worsens after return The expedition should keep moving. Failure changes what the party brings home. Route Drift During Expeditions Use route drift when the party’s intent weakens or conflicts. Signs include road signs become emotionally relevant a side path appears toward a remembered place maps show old memories instead of terrain one traveler hears a voice no one else hears lantern light bends toward a private fear the return phrase becomes hard to remember the route begins satisfying someone’s hidden wish Route drift should not be constant. Use it when the party is tired, afraid, divided, grieving, or vague. Return and Debrief Returning to Bellweather should have procedure. On return, ask Did the named travelers return? Did anyone extra return? Did anyone return missing something? Do witnesses recognize them? Do they recognize their own anchors? Did they bring back resources, clues, or contamination? What did the expedition teach the town? What did it teach the Violet? The return scene is where the expedition becomes civic consequence. Recognition Tests Use recognition tests for rescued persons, returned patrols, and anyone who came back under strange conditions. Tests should check continuity, not trivia. Examples full name address relationship to witnesses known scar or habit emotional reaction to a real person recognition of a personal object memory with imperfect human texture ability to contradict the role they appear to fulfill A false survivor may know the facts. A real person carries continuity. Expedition Consequences After the mission, update at least one of the following resource clocks public interpretation faction trust route stability Dream Pressure missing person status Violet Response Matrix Signal Leaks town rumors future mission options Even a successful expedition should change Bellweather. Core Principle An expedition is not over when the party finds the thing. It is over when Bellweather understands what came back.

title: Session Loop
summary: Provides the repeatable table procedure for Bellweather: city briefing, council slots, civic action resolution, mission framing, expedition play, resource upkeep, and return consequences.
body: Session Loop Bellweather works best with a repeatable session rhythm. The players should feel that each session is not just an isolated mystery, but another day of the town trying to remain itself. Use this loop as the default structure 1. City Briefing 2. Council Decision 3. Mission Framing 4. Expedition Play 5. Return and Consequence You can compress, expand, or skip steps as needed, but this loop keeps the campaign focused on both investigation and civic survival. How Long Is a Loop? A City Mode loop is the period between major civic decisions. It may represent one urgent day several days of pressure a week of rationing and repairs the time between expeditions one major crisis cycle Do not lock the loop to a strict calendar unless the situation calls for it. Use the loop as a pacing tool. The question is not always “How many hours passed?” Instead What did Bellweather have time, attention, labor, and trust to do before the next pressure arrived? 1. City Briefing Begin with the town. Present the current situation in practical terms. Good briefing ingredients one urgent resource issue one public rumor or interpretation problem one faction pressure one missing person or route concern one strange Violet development one possible expedition hook Examples Mercy Hospital has enough oil for one more night of full lamp use. The Gazette wants permission to print the word “miracle.” Three families demand a rescue mission to East Orchard Road. A ration ledger lists a household that no one remembers. Children at the schoolhouse drew the same locked room. The briefing should create choices, not just mood. Briefing Atmosphere and Civic Pressure The City Briefing should not feel like a sterile report. Bellweather is a living town. Its pressure should be visible before the council ever votes. Use the town’s current stress level, ignored problems, resource strain, and public interpretation to color the opening scene. Calm / Contained When the town is worried but still functional, show concern without disorder. Examples a few citizens waiting quietly outside the meeting room a mother asking whether the school will remain open a shopkeeper requesting clarification on ration rules a deputy whispering that people are asking about Eastgate hospital staff delivering a written request instead of interrupting clergy, union officers, or Gazette staff waiting to be called in The feeling should be tense, but orderly. People still believe someone is in charge. Stressed / Publicly Fraying When the town is stressed, let the pressure gather around the council. Examples a crowd in the hall demanding answers citizens barred from City Hall shouting outside families of the missing holding names and photographs union workers refusing night duty without oil priority parents demanding the schoolhouse be protected a preacher, rabbi, imam, or civic speaker warning that silence is making things worse Gazette reporters pressing for a public statement deputies visibly unsure whether to hold the line or let people in The feeling should be political and emotional. People are no longer only asking for reassurance. They are demanding decisions. Critical / Near Breaking When stress is critical, the council should feel surrounded by consequences. Examples City Hall doors locked against a crowd a faction leader forcing their way into the meeting a ration line turning violent nearby hospital bells ringing during the briefing a child witness brought in because adults ignored the issue too long a returned person appearing in the hall before anyone can explain them citizens chanting a dangerous phrase the Violet may answer a public notice torn down and rewritten by hand a trusted NPC accusing the council of choosing politics over people The feeling should be that delay has become action. If the council will not choose, the town will. Ignored Problems Should Find a Voice When the players repeatedly delay or ignore a problem, do not only mark a clock. Bring the problem into the room. A neglected issue may appear as an important NPC demanding action a grieving family asking why no one has gone after the missing a faction using the delay to gain influence a public rumor becoming accepted truth an unsafe interpretation spreading through sermons, newspapers, schools, or ration lines a resource worker refusing further service a retainer asking whether the town intends to abandon their people a child, patient, clerk, or witness revealing that the problem has already worsened This should not be used to punish players for prioritizing. City Mode is built on impossible triage. Instead, use ignored problems to show that every delayed issue continues living somewhere in Bellweather. Quick GM Prompt At the start of each City Briefing, ask 1. What did the council prioritize last loop? 2. What did they leave alone? 3. Who benefited from that choice? 4. Who suffered from that choice? 5. Who is now waiting outside the room? 2. Council Decision The players decide what Bellweather prioritizes. This may happen through an Emergency Civic Committee, informal faction council, player character authority, public meeting, or practical triage. Ask What problem gets attention this loop? What problem is delayed? Who will be angry about that? What public explanation is given? What does the town think this decision means? The decision should create both action and consequence. Council Slots Bellweather cannot do everything every loop. City Mode is not only about scarcity of food, oil, medicine, and labor. It is also about scarcity of attention. Default Slot Economy Each normal City Mode loop gives Bellweather three Council Slots . Each Council Slot can hold one Major Civic Action one attached Supporting Action optional character support optional resource spending A Major Civic Action is a town scale effort that requires meaningful authority, attention, labor, supplies, or public trust. A Supporting Action is a smaller attached effort that improves, protects, clarifies, or limits the Major Action. Not every Major Action creates an expedition. Expeditions happen only when the civic action must be resolved through direct character scale danger, investigation, travel, or contact with the Violet. Adjusting Slot Count Use three slots as the default. Adjust when the town’s condition changes Quiet / recovery loop 2 Council Slots Normal crisis loop 3 Council Slots Emergency mobilization 4 Council Slots, but mark Labor/Fatigue or Public Trust Collapse state 1–2 Council Slots until stabilized Extra slots are not free. If Bellweather over mobilizes, someone gets exhausted, frightened, coerced, or ignored. Council Slot Template Resolving Civic Actions Use this procedure when a Council Slot requires a City Mode roll. Use normal character rules when a specific character’s personal action is the dramatic focus. Use City Mode when Bellweather acts through institutions, crews, public systems, factions, retainers, or civic projects. City Mode uses d10 dice pools . Each die showing 8, 9, or 10 is a success. The size of the pool represents the town's current capacity, plus support, minus pressure. 1. Declare the Civic Action Name the action clearly. Define objective lead decision maker involved institution committed personnel committed resources public framing Violet risk return or completion condition A civic action declaration is not a magical command, but it matters because Bellweather is inside the Violet. A good declaration is specific, witnessed, limited, affirmative, grounded in records, clear about who is included, and careful about what it refuses to name. A bad declaration is vague, fear centered, punitive, category based, publicly amplified without safer language, or too broad for the Violet to interpret safely. 2. Choose the Civic Capacity Choose the most relevant town tracker. The tracker rating becomes the base d10 pool. Suggested civic capacities Survival — food, water, medicine, shelter, sanitation, emergency care Stability — law, morale, trust, public order, legitimacy Foothold — maps, lamps, gates, routes, boundaries, district integrity Understanding — research, interpretation, occult/scientific knowledge, pattern recognition Contact — expeditions, missing people, outside signals, diplomacy, scouting Adaptation — new protocols, invention, training, altered methods, resilience Return — endgame anchors, home doctrine, continuity, final civic consent Use a 0–5 rating. Example Foothold 3 means Bellweather has a functional but stressed route, lamp, and boundary system. A civic action to stabilize Eastgate begins with 3d10. 3. Add Character Support Each supporting character describes a concrete contribution and rolls an appropriate character skill. Examples A doctor supports hospital triage with Medicine. A Gazette editor supports public calm with Persuade or Psychology. A town clerk supports an identity audit with Library Use or Accounting. A deputy supports ration order enforcement with Intimidate, Law, or Firearms only if force is relevant. An engineer supports lamp grid repair with Mechanical Repair or Electrical Repair. A clergy member supports grief discipline with Persuade, Psychology, Theology, Occult, or another table approved faith/community skill. Support roll outcomes Regular success +1 die to the City Mode pool Hard success +2 dice Extreme success +2 dice and reduce one consequence if the City Mode roll partially succeeds Failure no bonus Pushed failure no bonus and add a complication, strain, or Violet risk tag Fumble 1 die or automatic complication Usually allow one lead character up to two supporting characters Allow more only if the action clearly has multiple workstreams. Cap the total support bonus at +3 dice unless the group is spending a major resource or making this problem the session's main civic focus. When Everyone Helps If every player wants to contribute, split the civic action into workstreams. Example Stabilize Eastgate after a failed patrol. Possible workstreams Route Records — clerk audits prior route declarations. Physical Barrier — engineers repair lamps, signs, and barricades. Public Calm — Gazette, clergy, or Town Hall explains the closure. Patrol Readiness — deputies organize guards and lantern bearers. Violet Interpretation — occultist reviews what phrase, dream, or fear triggered the drift. Each successful workstream may do one of the following add +1 die remove 1 penalty protect against one specific consequence unlock a safer declaration identify the likely Violet misread before the final roll Then roll one final City Mode pool. Do not roll five separate full City Mode actions unless the problem is meant to consume the whole cycle. 4. Commit Resources Spend relevant resources for extra strength. Spend 1 relevant resource +1 die or remove one penalty Spend 2 relevant resources +2 dice or protect against one likely consequence Spend a Surplus +2 dice or +1 die and prevent one related consequence Mark spent resources on their clocks. Resource spending should matter in the fiction. Examples Spend Oil/Light for lit routes, hospital operations, gate watches, or night patrols. Spend Food for morale, labor strength, refugee care, or public calm. Spend Medicine for triage, recovery, sleep ward care, or surgery. Spend Paper/Records for notices, route forms, ration cards, audits, or identity records. Spend Labor for repairs, patrols, construction, searches, hauling, or barricades. Spend Public Trust for unpopular orders, curfews, ration enforcement, assembly limits, or secrecy. A resource can also allow an action at all. If the hospital has no medicine, a medicine based civic action may be impossible without substitution, sacrifice, trade, or a dangerous Violet facing request. 5. Apply Pressure Subtract dice for active problems. Common penalties shortage public panic faction opposition rushed action weak identity vague declaration unsafe public language dangerous Violet interpretation exhausted labor missing records unreliable witnesses Usually remove dice rather than changing the target number. Keep success on 8–10 so the procedure stays fast. 6. Roll the Pool Roll the final d10 pool. Each 8, 9, or 10 is a success. 0 successes failure with consequence 1 success partial success or success with cost 2 successes clean success 3+ successes strong success with extra benefit 0 Successes — Failure With Consequence The action does not achieve its purpose, or achieves it in the wrong way. Mark a relevant tracker down, spend the committed resource anyway, and introduce a Violet misread, faction consequence, public panic, or new trouble. 1 Success — Partial or Costly Success The action works, but choose or assign a cost resource spent time lost retainer harmed public fear rises interpretation shifts route becomes unstable faction gains leverage the Violet learns an unhelpful lesson 2 Successes — Clean Success The action achieves its purpose without major new harm. Update the relevant tracker or stabilize the pressure. 3+ Successes — Strong Success The action succeeds and creates an additional benefit extra information improved public trust reduced panic preserved resource better route faction support useful doctrine temporary Surplus reduced Dream Pressure a safer future declaration Town Stress and Complications Town Stress does not only make failure more likely. It makes success messier. If Town Stress is low, ignore 1s unless the scene calls for danger. If Town Stress is high, each die showing 1 creates a complication even if the action succeeds. A complication may be rumor spread public fear retainer injury faction backlash resource waste Violet misread witness exhaustion record corruption dream echo unsafe phrase repeated publicly A stressed town can still succeed. It just pays more for success. 7. Update the Town After the roll, update relevant civic tracker resource clocks public interpretation faction position Violet Response Matrix rumors, notices, or future trouble Ask two final questions What does this teach the public? What does this teach the Violet? Practical success does not automatically mean metaphysical success. Resource Clocks Use resource clocks instead of exact inventory unless the table wants detailed scarcity. Suggested resource clocks Food / Grain Oil / Light Medicine Salt / Preservation Livestock Paper / Records Fuel / Coal Labor / Fatigue Public Trust A 6 segment clock works well. When a clock reaches 6, trigger a civic pressure. Examples Food crisis ration unrest, theft, black market, hunger dreams. Oil crisis route failure, hospital darkness, night panic. Medicine crisis triage deaths, sleep ward failures, desperate Violet requests. Paper crisis records degrade, route forms are rationed, identity anchors weaken. Labor crisis accidents, resentment, exhausted witnesses, failed patrols. Trust crisis faction revolt, underground sermons, rumor dominance. After the crisis triggers, reset the clock to 3 if partially stabilized, or keep it at 6 if unresolved. Surplus Surplus is a temporary advantage. If a resource is in unusually good shape, mark it as Surplus. Examples Food Surplus Oil Surplus Medical Surplus Paper Surplus Labor Surplus Trust Surplus A Surplus can be spent once for +2 dice +1 die and prevent a related consequence allow a major project without ticking the resource clock cancel a related Critical penalty for one action Then remove the Surplus. Surplus should feel valuable, but not permanent. Shortage If a resource clock is critical or maxed, it creates penalties. Strained no penalty, but spending is risky. Critical 1 die to actions relying on that resource. Crisis the action may be impossible without substitute supply, sacrifice, trade, or a dangerous Violet facing request. Examples Oil Critical 1 to expedition, gate, hospital night, and lamp grid actions. Paper Critical 1 to records, public notices, route declarations, and identity audits. Trust Critical 1 to curfews, rationing, service bans, and public language campaigns. Labor Critical 1 to patrols, construction, repairs, searches, hauling, and barricades. End of Loop Upkeep At the end of each City Mode loop, ask what Bellweather relied on. Mark 1 pressure on each resource that was heavily used Food , if the loop covered feeding crowds, refugees, labor crews, or ration strain. Oil / Light , if routes, gates, hospital wards, or night patrols were active. Medicine , if there were injuries, illness, surgery, sleep cases, or panic casualties. Paper / Records , if the town issued notices, route forms, ration cards, audits, or identity records. Labor , if crews worked dangerous shifts, patrols, repairs, searches, or construction. Public Trust , if the town used secrecy, force, rationing, bans, curfews, or controversial orders. If a resource was protected, replenished, or carefully managed during the loop, do not mark pressure. If a resource was the focus of a successful civic action, reduce its pressure by 1 or create a temporary Surplus. The GM does not need to run an economy every loop. Ask what the town leaned on. 3. Mission Framing Convert the chosen priority into playable scenes. If the priority is civic, frame a City Mode scene. If the priority requires leaving safety, frame an expedition. If the priority is public meaning, frame a meeting, sermon dispute, Gazette debate, school assembly, hospital crisis, or faction confrontation. For expeditions, define party members destination purpose route witnesses anchors return phrase expected completion condition For civic actions, define responsible institution public declaration resources spent opposition tracker at stake possible misinterpretation Expedition Triggers One loop usually produces zero or one expedition. Two expeditions in one loop means Bellweather is stretching itself. Three expeditions means something is going badly wrong. An expedition is triggered only when the civic action cannot be completed from town and must be resolved through direct danger, investigation, travel, or contact with the Violet. Before leaving City Mode, define destination objective personnel route declaration lantern or anchor assignments public framing return condition what will happen if the party does not return The city decides what must be done. The expedition discovers what that decision costs. 4. Expedition Play Run the main body of the session using normal character scale play. This may include investigation interviews archives travel route drift combat negotiation dream contact medical crisis faction conflict resource recovery rescue Continuity Stress Keep the ordinary world visible. A strange event lands harder when it violates something concrete a ledger, a bell, a ration line, a route form, a school roster, a hospital chart, a family photograph. 5. Return and Consequence End by bringing the result back to Bellweather. Ask What did the players learn? What did they fail to learn? Who was helped? Who was harmed? What resource changed? What rumor spreads? What faction shifts? What tracker moves? What did the public now believe? What did the Violet now learn? This step is essential. Without return and consequence, Bellweather becomes a backdrop. With it, the town becomes the campaign. Between Session Updates Between sessions, the GM can quietly update clocks faction intentions public rumor missing person lists dream reports resource strain Violet response patterns future trouble escalation Do not overprepare every possible branch. Prepare pressure. Let the players decide where the pressure breaks. Session Opening Template Example Session Closing Template Example Pacing Guidance A strong session usually needs one town scale decision one focused mission or civic conflict one meaningful clue one human cost one Violet misinterpretation or response one consequence that changes the next session Do not try to resolve every tracker every session. Pick the pressure that matters now. Core Principle Start with Bellweather. Choose what the town can bear. Leave with purpose. Return with consequence.

title: Alternate Game Systems
summary: Explains how to adapt Bellweather / The Violet Edge to other rules systems, including a fantasy-medieval 5e reskin, while preserving the campaign’s core themes of civic survival, interpretive horror, and teaching the Violet what care means.
body: Alternate Game Systems Bellweather / The Violet Edge is designed primarily for a Call of Cthulhu style investigative horror campaign with a light civic survival overlay. That is the cleanest default because the campaign is about fragile people, investigation, stress, mystery, injury, public fear, and the cost of understanding. However, the setting can be adapted to other systems if the GM preserves the core engine A community trapped in the care of something vast, sincere, and dangerously literal must survive long enough to teach it what care and home mean. The rules can change. That thesis should not. What the System Must Support A good rules engine for Bellweather should support at least some of the following investigation ordinary or vulnerable characters stress, fear, sanity, corruption, or identity pressure dangerous travel social conflict resource scarcity faction pressure consequences beyond combat mystery without guaranteed answers characters changing under pressure community scale decisions If the chosen system only supports tactical combat and character advancement, the GM will need to build more of the civic and interpretive horror layer by hand. Default Mode Call of Cthulhu Style Play Use this mode when you want investigators instead of superheroes mystery and danger fragile bodies sanity / Continuity Stress social pressure lethal mistakes slow understanding horror rooted in ordinary people The civic layer sits above the character rules. The players still investigate, make skill rolls, suffer stress, take wounds, and solve mysteries, but their choices also move town trackers and shape public interpretation. 5e / Fantasy Medieval Reskin Bellweather can become a fantasy town, fortified market city, border duchy, lantern road settlement, or temple guild city displaced into the Violet Edge. In this version, translate the 1930s civic institutions into fantasy equivalents. 1930s Bellweather Fantasy Reskin Town Hall City council, ducal court, guild council Gazette Town criers, broadsheets, bardic messengers, temple notices Mercy Hospital Temple infirmary, plague house, healer’s guild Schoolhouse / Library Scriptorium, academy, archive, chantry school Lampworks Lantern guild, ward stone masons, alchemists, artificers Rail depot Caravan gate, river docks, portal road, trade causeway Eastgate route office Gatehouse, road wardens, charter office Deputies / police Watch, reeves, sworn lantern guard Churches / faith groups Temples, shrines, saint cults, ancestor halls Union rolls Guild rosters, craft rolls, militia musters Ration office Granary office, tithe barn, guild storehouse Cemetery records Crypt ledgers, ancestor stones, death bells Fantasy Violet Logic The Violet still should not become a normal god, demon, fey court, or outer plane villain by default. Those are interpretations the town may impose on it. In a fantasy skin, the Violet may be mistaken for a god a demon the Feywild a divine trial a saint’s miracle a curse a dead kingdom a dream realm a living plane an ancient compact The GM should preserve the same truth The Violet is not granting wishes. It is completing contexts. 5e Tone Warning Standard 5e characters are much more durable and powerful than Call of Cthulhu investigators. This changes the campaign. If using 5e, consider lower starting level gritty resting slower healing stronger exhaustion rules corruption / stress track reduced access to easy resurrection limits on teleportation and planar escape town scale consequences that cannot be solved by one spell monsters that are interpretive problems, not only hit point bags The campaign should not become Kill the Violet. The Violet is not a boss monster. 5e Character Roles Strong fantasy equivalents include watch captain temple healer guild clerk lantern warden road ranger hedge occultist archivist plague doctor oathbound knight disgraced noble miller’s child town reeve apprentice artificer gravekeeper shrine speaker The best characters still have civic responsibility. They should be tied to people, records, institutions, roads, debts, duties, and grief. Keeping the Civic Layer No matter the system, keep the civic layer. Track Survival Stability Foothold Understanding Contact Adaptation Return Dream Pressure Public Interpretation Violet Response The system handles character action. The civic layer handles what those actions teach the town and the Violet. Other Good System Fits Trail of Cthulhu / GUMSHOE Strong fit for clue driven investigation and horror. Use the civic layer for town pressure. Vaesen Strong fit for folklore, community, and dangerous interpretation. Better for a mythic rural version. Mothership Mothership is a strong fit for survival horror if Bellweather is reskinned as a border station, colony ship, orbital refinery, agricultural relay, or company town that suffers a catastrophic warp / skipspace incident and emerges inside a violet nebula, extradimensional medium, or impossible signal field. In this version, the Violet is not a frontier beyond the roads. It is the Drift outside the hull a responsive medium that hears crew distress, maintenance requests, life support alarms, personnel records, prayers, dreams, station protocols, and emergency broadcasts. The core horror remains the same Something vast is trying to keep the community alive before it understands life, consent, identity, or home. In 1930s Bellweather, the dangerous phrase is We need help. In Mothership, it becomes Keep us alive. The Violet Drift may answer with locked bulkheads, emergency sedation, cryosleep, nutrient lines, replacement crew, perfect life support with unknown biological cost, cloned loved ones, or rescue vessels that never existed. 1930s Bellweather Mothership / Sci Fi Skin Town Hall Station command / admin core Gazette Comms office / internal bulletin / station feed Mercy Hospital Medbay / cryo ward / bio lab Schoolhouse / Library Training deck / archive / colony classroom Lampworks Reactor shop / maintenance bay / beacon techs Rail depot Docking ring / shuttle bay / cargo spine Eastgate Airlock gate / docking corridor / EVA lock Old roads Maintenance tunnels / jump corridors / sensor paths Church basements Multi faith chapel / rec room / civilian shelter Municipal records Personnel manifests / station logs / access records Farms / orchards Hydroponics / protein vats / agri rings Old wall / boundary Hull plating / radiation shield / station perimeter Lanterns Beacons / suit lights / transponders / locator tags Route declarations EVA plans / corridor routing / mission packets The Mothership version should lean into stress, isolation, body horror, procedural failure, and the terror of systems that optimize survival without understanding personhood. Delta Green Strong fit for a modern federal containment version, but the town civic heart may become more institutional and conspiratorial. Blades in the Dark / Forged in the Dark Strong fit if the campaign emphasizes factions, clocks, expeditions, and town projects. Horror becomes pressure clock driven. Powered by the Apocalypse / Brindlewood style Games Strong fit for collaborative mystery, tone first play, and community pressure if the GM is comfortable with more interpretive mechanics. Poor Fit Warning A system is a poor fit if it assumes combat solves most problems characters are isolated adventurers mysteries exist only as puzzles community consequences do not matter the world waits passively for the party power escalation is the main reward Bellweather works best when the question is not Can the party defeat the threat? but What does the town become while trying to survive it? Core Conversion Principle When converting Bellweather, preserve these five things 1. The Violet answers contexts, not wishes. 2. Public meaning changes reality. 3. Names, records, witnesses, and routes matter. 4. The town is a character. 5. The endgame is teaching home, not finding a portal.

title: Civic Actions
summary: Defines the major civic action categories Bellweather can pursue, explains how town-scale action differs from character-scale action, and gives examples of actions, declarations, risks, and outcomes.
body: Civic Actions Civic actions are the town scale things Bellweather can attempt when the players ask What does the town do? A civic action is not one person making one roll. It is a coordinated effort by institutions, crews, factions, retainers, public offices, and named decision makers. Civic actions are chosen during City Mode and resolved through the Session Loop when the outcome is uncertain. They are how Bellweather turns fear into procedure. What a Civic Action Is A civic action should define 1. Decision Maker — who authorizes or sponsors it. 2. Objective — what the action is meant to accomplish. 3. Personnel — who carries it out. 4. Resources — what supplies, authority, labor, records, or public trust are committed. 5. Public Framing — what the town believes the action means. 6. Violet Risk — what vague need, fear, or phrase could be answered badly. 7. Return or Completion Condition — what counts as success, failure, or safe closure. A weak civic action says We make the roads safer. A stronger civic action says Eastgate will suspend unrecorded departures, audit the last three route ledgers, assign two named witnesses to each patrol, and stabilize the Crow Mill route before another rescue is authorized. The difference matters. The Violet hears gaps. The Eight Major Civic Action Categories 1. Investigate the Mystery Use when Bellweather needs to understand what is happening. Examples compare altered records interview returned people study a Violet supplied building investigate shared dreams review old boundary maps identify a repeated public phrase collect witness statements analyze a new manifestation Typical capacity Understanding Common risks dangerous conclusions spread too early investigators mistake one pattern for the whole truth public language becomes contaminated the Violet answers the investigation method, not the goal 2. Survive Until Tomorrow Use when the town needs immediate food, water, medicine, shelter, heat, sanitation, or emergency care. Examples ration food open a shelter triage the hospital repair a water line secure a cold room allocate coal and oil protect vulnerable residents organize sanitation crews Typical capacity Survival Common risks scarcity becomes a moral hierarchy rationing creates public anger the Violet tries to provide the missing resource too literally desperate people begin making unsafe requests 3. Solidify the Foothold Use when Bellweather needs to make a place, route, district, or procedure more stable. Examples reinforce Eastgate repair ward lamps update maps and signs name unstable streets establish route offices protect district boundaries create witness posts mark safe return points Typical capacity Foothold Common risks over defining a boundary excludes people outside it signs become too authoritative one district is protected while another is abandoned the Violet interprets “safety” as containment 4. Govern the Town Use when the problem is order, legitimacy, morale, public language, law, rumors, or faction conflict. Examples issue a public notice hold a council hearing impose or lift curfew negotiate with a faction regulate unsafe assembly correct a rumor define ration rules decide who has authority Typical capacity Stability Common risks control replaces care public trust is spent too fast a faction exploits the decision the Violet learns that safety means obedience 5. Expand the Map Use when Bellweather needs to know more of the Violet Edge, establish routes, scout resources, or confirm what still exists. Examples authorize a scouting patrol confirm a farm or mill map a boundary site test a road locate missing landmarks mark a new route identify other settlements expand lamp coverage Typical capacity Contact or Foothold Common risks route drift lost retainers dangerous discoveries public hope outruns proof the Violet fills map blanks with emotionally useful places 6. Build Power Use when Bellweather develops new methods, training, infrastructure, rituals, tools, or countermeasures. Examples train witness clerks standardize route declarations repair generators build lantern housings develop dream protocols create recognition tests organize retainer squads improve hospital procedures establish communication relays Typical capacity Adaptation Common risks efficiency replaces consent systems become rigid tools are treated as wards instead of clarifications the Violet learns the form but not the human limit 7. Make Contact Use when the town attempts to reach missing people, other settlements, entities, mediums, outside signals, or the Violet itself. Examples contact a missing patrol establish radio procedure parley with a returned person question a Violet touched medium send a named rescue party attempt dream contact communicate with another settlement test whether the outside world can hear Typical capacity Contact or Understanding Common risks the contact answers too well grief overwhelms specificity unknown persons enter the town without identity protocol the Violet treats contact as invitation 8. Choose What the Town Becomes Use when the issue is not practical survival alone, but identity. Examples decide who counts as Bellweather accept or reject a changed returnee preserve an old district name teach safer public language choose mercy over efficiency define what “home” must include decide whether Violet supplied infrastructure may remain determine what the town is willing to become to survive Typical capacity Return , Stability , or Understanding Common risks the town teaches the Violet an exclusionary answer public meaning hardens around fear a moral compromise becomes civic doctrine Bellweather survives by becoming less itself Civic Reinforcement of Travel Eastgate and other boundary checkpoints become more than barricades. They become reality stabilizing offices. A proper departure is recorded by a clerk, repeated by the travelers, witnessed by named people, and entered into the town’s route ledger. This gives the Violet fewer gaps to complete incorrectly. The clerk matters because the clerk turns intent into record. The witness matters because the witness turns record into relationship. The gate matters because the gate turns relationship into return. A route declaration should ideally include full names of all travelers destination purpose permitted route expected return gate lantern numbers assigned witnesses departure time return phrase physical tokens carried known risks or prohibited interpretations The route is then read aloud before departure. The travelers confirm it. The witnesses confirm it. The clerk records it. The gate holds it. This may look bureaucratic from the outside, but in Bellweather bureaucracy becomes survival magic by another name. Route Office Procedure A mature Bellweather route office may use this procedure before any expedition. 1. Name the Travelers Each traveler speaks their full name and confirms their role. 2. Name the Destination The clerk reads the destination as written on the route form. 3. Name the Purpose The party states the mission in one sentence. 4. Name the Return The return gate, return phrase, and expected time are spoken aloud. 5. Assign Witnesses At least two named witnesses confirm they will remember the route until it is closed. 6. Record Lanterns and Tokens Lantern numbers, route tokens, maps, and personal anchors are logged. 7. State the Limits The clerk reads any prohibited interpretations no substitution, no unpaid tender, no dream custody, no replacement survivors, no altered return. 8. Close the Declaration The travelers, witnesses, and clerk repeat the return phrase. A route is not closed until the travelers return, are confirmed dead, are declared missing by civic vote, or the witnesses are formally released from holding the route.
